Details: A full-scale study [2175] of a plant treating reclaimed domestic wastewater found biological filtration to be ineffective for removing most per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) with some success in removing perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) (48 percent). In one sampling event, PFOS was removed to below the limits of reporting (0.3 ng/L). A second full-scale study [2851] found 89 to 93 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), PFOS, perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), and perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) and 52 percent removal of PFBA. The same study found increases in concentrations of PFHxS. Moderate removal of perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA) (57 percent) and PFOS (63 percent) was observed in the biological filtration step at a pilot-scale potable reuse facility, but removal of shorter chain PFASs was much lower (-59 to 25 percent) [2659]. Removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA) and PFOS in a full-scale, biologically active carbon filter treating surface water was inconsistent, with some samples showing reductions of PFOS, but others showing increases [2668].
